title: Faith Without Works Is Dead
subtitle: No partiality, the royal law of love, and faith demonstrated by action

heb.paragraph: Do not hold the faith with partiality. The term literally means receiving the face.
The royal law is love your neighbor as yourself. It is royal because it is the king law.
Mercy triumphs over judgment. Those who show mercy will receive mercy.

refs.note: You shall do no injustice in court. You shall not be partial.
The LORD your God is not partial and takes no bribe.
Blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy.

notes.note: The hypothetical scenario exposes endemic sin. Fine clothing signals wealth; shabby dress signals poverty.
God chose the poor to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom.
The royal law is love your neighbor. Partiality violates this law.
Calvin notes that favoritism inverts God values. God chose the poor; the church honors the rich.
The law unity means partial obedience is disobedience.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The term synagogue here may refer to a Christian assembly.
The royal law is royal because it rules over other commands.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo emphasizes that favoritism is not a minor social faux pas but a violation of core Christian ethics.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney connects James to OT prophetic critique: Amos condemned those who trampled the poor.

hist.historical: James writes to 'the twelve tribes in the Dispersion' — Jewish Christians scattered throughout the Roman Empire after persecution in Jerusalem (Acts 8:1; 11:19). The letter reflects the earliest stage of Christianity when believers still gathered in synagogues (2:2, Greek synagōgē). James, the brother of Jesus, led the Jerusalem church (Acts 15; Gal 2:9) until his martyrdom in AD 62. His letter addresses practical concerns: economic disparity between rich and poor, the relationship between faith and works, and the power of the tongue. The wisdom tradition of Judaism pervades the letter, echoing Proverbs and Jesus' Sermon on the Mount. The agricultural imagery (1:11; 3:12; 5:7) and references to early and late rains reflect the Palestinian context.
hist.context: James addresses the sin of favoritism. A rich man enters in fine clothing; a poor man in shabby clothes. The rich is honored; the poor is told to stand or sit on the floor.

heb.paragraph: What good is it if someone claims to have faith but has no works? Can that faith save him?
Faith without works is dead. James uses three terms: useless, dead, and barren.
Was not Abraham justified by works? James uses justify to mean demonstrated.

refs.note: Abraham believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.
The Aqedah: Abraham offering of Isaac.
Rahab hid the spies and was saved.
Paul: a person is justified by faith apart from works of the law.

notes.note: Faith without works cannot save. The example is devastating: saying peace, be warm while doing nothing.
Show me your faith apart from works. Even demons believe and shudder.
Abraham was justified by works when he offered Isaac. His faith was completed by works.
Rahab was justified by works. Faith without works is dead like a body without spirit.
Calvin harmonizes James and Paul. Paul speaks of justification before God; James speaks of justification before others.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb justified can mean declared righteous or shown to be righteous.
And he was called a friend of God. This title is from Isaiah 41:8.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo argues that James and Paul address different opponents. Both affirm: genuine faith transforms behavior.

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney emphasizes that James defines faith by its fruit. Dead faith is mere assent; living faith obeys.

hist.context: This is the most debated passage in James. James insists that genuine faith produces works. Abraham faith was completed by his works when he offered Isaac.

themes.note: Faith is explicit throughout. Justice is strong in the partiality section.

rows.label: vv. 8-13
rows.text: The royal law and the law unity

rows.label: vv. 21-26
rows.text: Abraham, Rahab, and the body analogy

lit.note: Chapter 2 has two movements: no partiality and faith-works. Both address what genuine faith looks like in practice.

discourse.title: James 2 — Partiality and Faith Without Works
discourse.thesis: Showing partiality to the rich violates the royal law. Faith without works is dead — even demons believe.

nodes.label: No partiality (2:1-7)
nodes.text: Show no partiality as you hold the faith. If a rich man enters and you seat him well while telling the poor man to stand, have you not made distinctions and become judges with evil thoughts? Did not God choose the poor to be rich in faith?

nodes.label: Royal law (2:8-13)
nodes.text: If you fulfill the royal law — 'Love your neighbor as yourself' — you do well. But partiality is sin. Whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles at one point is guilty of all. Speak and act as those to be judged under the law of liberty. Judgment is without mercy to one who showed no mercy.

nodes.label: Faith without works (2:14-17)
nodes.text: What good is it if someone claims faith but has no works? Can such faith save? If you say 'Go in peace, be warmed and filled' but give nothing, what good is that? Faith by itself, without works, is dead.

nodes.label: Show me your faith (2:18-19)
nodes.text: Someone will say, 'You have faith and I have works.' Show me your faith apart from works, and I will show you my faith by my works. Even the demons believe — and shudder.

nodes.label: Abraham and Rahab (2:20-26)
nodes.text: Abraham was justified by works when he offered Isaac — faith was completed by works. Rahab was likewise justified by works when she received the spies. As the body without spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead.

discourse.note: James 2:14-26 is the most debated passage in the letter, often seen as tension with Paul. Both oppose dead faith; they define 'works' differently.
